story_text.value:
I am building Promptster - an AI fluency platform that helps level up engineering organizations. Engineering managers invite their teammates and Promptster analyzes the engineers work with ai coding tools (claude code, codex, cursor, copilot). The manager receives a team-aggregate dashboard where they can roll-out certain practices / skill to their whole team. Each IC receives their own dashboard where they can see their fluency statistics, skill usage, context management, and a DORA dashboard.

We have an open-source rubric that tries to answer, what are best practices for Claude Code (and AI coding agents in-general), would love to hear thoughts and feedback on it. The question is a bit abstract, but I tried my best to figure out practices that I use myself and the top minds agree on.

https://github.com/promptster-ai/rubric

title.value: Ask HN: What makes someone good at using Claude Code?

story_text.value:
Hello HN, I'm here to push yet another AI coding agent into your faces. The industry definitely needed one more.

Everything worthwhile I've built in 30+ years of C++ (JUCE, the Tracktion DAW, the Cmajor language) started when I got annoyed with a tool I had to use, and decided to have a go myself. I had that moment with coding agents - it's clear the rest of my career involves staring at one of these things all day, and I find a terminal a horrible UX for it. So I've spent the last ~6 months building a tool that suited the way I like to work.

The core idea: the session is a document, not a log file. Each conversation is a Yjs CRDT tree — any point can branch into sub-threads (recursively), and you can drill down, backtrack, edit, undo/redo, and inspect everything, including tool calls, approvals, and the raw context JSON going to the model. The UI lays it out in Finder-style Miller columns rather than a collapsible chat doom-scroll.

Because it's a CRDT under a local web server, multiple clients can attach P2P to a live session: the native desktop app, a browser tab, or your phone from across the network — all synced. Run the headless server on the box where the code lives, view it from wherever.

Almost everything is a JavaScript extension: the tools (read/write/bash/etc.), the LLM loop strategies, slash commands, and their UIs. You can inspect, fork, or replace any of them. If your orchestration idea needs some custom UI, this is meant to be a platform for that.

Tech: Go backend, Wails for windowing (no Electron), plain type-checked JS (JSDoc + strict linting, no build step), Yjs for the documents. It talks to the usual model zoo — Claude (CLI or API), OpenAI/Codex, Gemini, Ollama, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, etc.

The app is AGPLv3; the extension SDK and bundled extensions are Apache-2.0, so you can build closed-source extensions with no copyleft strings. No signup, no telemetry of your code - it only talks to the model provider you configure.

Fair warning: this is a beta, and is a one-man side project, and hasn't yet had a proper kicking from the real world. Constructive brutality welcome!

https://juggler.studio

title.value: Show HN: Juggler – an open-source GUI coding agent, by the creator of JUCE

story_text.value:
I built r3 to scratch my own itch when reviewing long design doc and planning doc from AI. The chat interface is inherently bad at tracking multiple pieces of feedback across different parts of a document, so I created a lightweight local web UI to organize the review process.

I have been using it on my own projects for one week, and it has made giving structured feedback to AI much easier. I thought others working with AI coding agents might find it useful too.

I'd love to hear what you think and any feedback you have!

title.value: Show HN: R3 – A Local Code Review Tool for You and Your AI Agent

hits.author: rickye26
